item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. I had been thinking about a similar-but-worse idea (surface tension stops working around Scranton Reality Anchors), but never figured out where it would go from there. This worked really well. You had good tests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all apologies for necroposting.

Just wanted to add that we don't even know for sure if there even was a big bang nor do we know the head death would be the actual end of the universe, we are merely hypothesizing all that based on the cosmic background radiation, the current expansion of the universe and the increase of entropy.

But what if the Big Bounce is the correct answer?

Conservation of Entropy might very well be true and Carnot Convection is actually happening, it's just taking a rrreeeaaallllllyyy lllooonnnggg time.

Pre Event 4039-1 still violates the second law of thermodynamics tho, I demand an abstract of Dr. Remi's lecture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Entropy still varies in this universe, apparently, it just oscillates around some constant depending on how fast this Carnot convection was. Perhaps some very massive stars can burn faster than Carnot convection and it's their end products that slowly change back into hydrogen over time.

This could also imply it doesn't have a beginning but extends infinitely into the past with a fixed proportion of heavier elements (and entropy, really) that would be heterogeneously distributed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well if you're terrified of the idea the universe ending, just remember that the whole theory we have of the heat death is just a placeholder because we don't know exactly where energy comes from or how to better efficiently generate it. Its basically the modern equivalent of people being afraid of falling into the void if they go off the edge of the map.
The downside to this is that it means the true horror of life is actually that it doesn't stop, or can be stopped, since "matter cannot be created or destroyed". Life just continues in a endless parade of suffering with no escape but to struggle against it.

Nhilism is luxury we can't afford. However I think your on to something about a malevolent entity using entropy to kill the world. do you mind if I reference your entry in my own work? I was thinking of making my own SCP based on the idea of the jealous nothingness before time versus the endless chaos that birthed the world

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first instinct was a strong +1 but after seeing here the logical implications I didn't think about at first, I'm debating undoing my vote or even downvoting. This is a fantastic notion that's right up my alley but I think there's a lot more you can and *should* do with the idea, including explaining how life as we know it could even come to be in a universe where total entropy never increases.

Also there's an instance of "anesthetized" that surely should be "amnesticized" but I'm hesitant to change it since it's not exactly a spelling or grammar fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally agree with ARD that

I'm not feeling any sense of frisson from the idea that entropy was created by God to halt human progress.

This scp should have expanded more and dug deeper, better getting us thrilled.

-1.

PS: In Chinese branch we are now having heated discussion on the concept of this anomalous “entropy”. Sadly, negative comments are dominant…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I understand that many people's suspension of disbelief may be completely broken by the idea of the old universe being in any way similar to our own despite its fundamentally different laws of physics, I still think the concept and execution are quite interesting. Overall, a really enjoyable read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was going to give this the standard +1 for having read it, but after reading the comments I am giving it with gusto.
The idea that god themself is like, we done here, yeah, nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giving real-world phenomena anomalous explanations is such a great hook that it's hard for me not to love this.

The idea that God said "okay kids you've had your fun" and shut us down was a touch too much for me, but there's enough good stuff in here for it to be an easy upvote regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely well written, and while I initially had upvoted, I switched to a novote instead after thinking more about it. The idea that entropy itself is an anomaly is undoubtedly interesting, as is the idea of the entirety of the Second Law of Dynamics being a cover-up for this fact, but the idea that entropy wasn't always part of the world introduces several logical problems that bring me out of it. Had entropy not always existed, but rather be introduced later on during humanity's development (which couldn't even occur in a world without entropy, as it would be impossible for humans to exist without it), the world would have been incredibly different than it is, and while that is intriguing on it's own, it breaks my suspension of disbelief in this article.

item.title: "Enough" in Lojban
item.content:encoded:
rau
http://vlasisku.lojban.org/rau

mi nelci le se ciska gi'e jdice co dunda lo su'u zanru
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Besides any problem with life having existed without entropy, my biggest issue with this is that I felt less existential dread from reading this than from reading my physics textbook entry on entropy. Perhaps that's to be blamed on already being aware of the Heat Death of the universe and such things, but that just means this article isn't entertaining after the first time, which is another minus. The "this anomalous thing is normalized like 8900-EX" thing doesn't work either, because that article had the foundation expressing disgust and horror at how the world had changed, which isn't felt here.

On the other hand, I am introgued by the idea of the universe working with the Carnot convection. After all, it doesn't really imply no variations on entropy in a closed system, but that they can't remain past a certain period of time, which reminds me of the uncertainty principle, which would mean this hypothetical world has quantum effects on a macroscopic scale, which would make for one REALLY weird world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Believe it or not, this idea (that entropy in the way we know it today was made by humans in a cosmic-level "oopsie") is a 001 proposal over in the French branch. Dr. Johannes' Proposal: The Xibalba Project. Almost the same coverup, too. For that same reason I'm an iffy novote because I'm glad the idea made it into the English site but since it's already been done it feels…reposti.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the idea is really cool, and the article is well written, there is one part that really irks me.

The implication that Carnot Convection used to allow stars to burn forever would mean that elements heavier than hydrogen, helium, and lithium would not exist, as any element heavier than that required stars to burn out and explode, with the force of the explosion fusing atoms into heavier and heavier elements, as well as spreading them across the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is exactly what an intelectual who grew up under the effects of SCP-4039 and Operation Gaslight would think. If you are interested in learning more about how things worked before Event 4039-1, you can attend Dr. Remi's 73 part lecture provided at Site 19 entitled "Wait huh what do you mean there just 'didn't used to be entropy' that is nuts: Yeah we know but trust us, anomalous physics only get weirder from here."

Dr. Remi is a great historian and physicist. He is not very good at naming things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a nasty problem with this one, the previous state of the universe described is either one that prevents any work from happening, or it loops on a short time scale. Either way it's one that is utterly incompatible with life. The big bang, wouldn't among other issues.

I like the idea that entropy is anomalous, but it also means that effects of time as we know them used to not exist. Entropy can be seen as the way of distinguishing the direction of time, removing it removes everything that depends on time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a physics major, the idea that entropy itself is effectively anomalous, and that the Second Law of Thermodynamics is a gigantic Foundation cover-up for that, hit me like a ton of bricks. Confident +1 to you, my good man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, author of the 001 French article here! It's okay, you didn't know about it when you wrote yours, and it's kind of an obvious idea in the first place, so I don't really mind. I only hope it won't end up being so popular people end up telling me I plagiarised you, haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have never explored the French site but I see their proposal now (which is amazing by the way).

I promise I was unaware of theirs when I wrote mine. I hate that it now sort of looks like I copied their idea. :/

I have messaged that author (who seems still very active) as well as asking in the forums about if this is fine or if this is a problem that needs to be sorted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for hard-science SCPs +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehh, I think "unintuitive reading of semi obscure poem" is not a great way to title a Skip.

LUCKILY I realized I could turn one of my boring and literal title choices into something more interesting. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a big idea - enough to be an 001 proposal (take that as a compliment)

It's worth noting that this is extremely similar in concept to a French 001 proposal originally posted in 2015.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of had to double-check since I thought I had read it wrong the first couple of times. I would suggest try keeping the original title, but adding something to it to avoid people into thinking that they read it wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your reaction is totally legit and i take your 001 comment as a huge compliment.

I wanted to clarify that the Foundation isn't throwing up their hands. They made it a Chi priority (which I invented but means "maximum importance but minimal urgency." They even are using Operation Crowdsource (another thing I made up but it's so serious and they have had so little progress that they are getting outside sources to work on the problem unknowingly….my ead cannon is that this is almost never used).

I could have gone more into their "ongoing" research, but I wrote it with the idea that 100 years of active and motivated research has lead to absolutely zero leads. If it weren't killing the universe, maybe they would have some hard decisions like in 8900-EX to accept it, but as it stands they will never accept it…even if they can't change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So not really, and I struggled over the title. I was inspired by Robert Frost’s poem, Mending Fence which starts with “something there is that doesn’t love a wall”. I think that might have been commentary about how nature abhors separateness and division, but I took it as nature abhors building.

I wondered what a thing that hates walls would look like and decided it was “entropy itself given form”

I toyed with other names in the draft forum and chat, but didn’t get any good feedback. Other options were The Second Law of Thermodynamics, or Entropy, or Thermodynamic Equilibrium, or Heat Death or Heat Death Of The Universe

Thoughts? I didn’t intend to change the title after posting but if it is causing issues I will consider it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a little confused by the title. Was it intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: as an astronomy and physics nerd, I loved this. I liked the addendum with each object(s) being affected by the entropy. The fact that things we know or have "explained" are actually SCPs adds nice realism and fear into it. Really cool ideas. Also, entropy is a cool word. +1 :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’ve always been a fan of 8900-EX and the general concept of the Foundation going to extreme lengths to preserve normalcy, even to the point of redefining what is normal, and I think the “Enough.” bit was a great touch that gave this article the narrative backbone to stand apart from its predecessors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, while I complimented the author on its brevity, you're right that swung hard in the opposite direction this could be an awesome 001 proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also respect the idea and execution of what's present, but I don't think it goes far enough. This is a big idea - enough to be an 001 proposal (take that as a compliment) - but it's expressed as a mid-length article, and doesn't delve into any deeper philosophising about the ramifications of this thing. You could argue that this approach would be appropriate to the Foundationverse and it purely boils down to personal taste, which is fair, but I also don't think this is in character for the Foundation. If you compare it to SCP-8900-EX, while it doesn't go into much detail, it's made clear that accepting the colours of everything changing was a hard decision and large amounts of time and resources were dedicated to restoring normalcy. This, despite having infinitely more serious consequences, seems to have been met with a 'welp I guess this is just how things are now' (with nobody ever revisiting that assumption in 100 years).

Novoted, but reluctantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. It's definitely well-done for what was intended, and the result is respectable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is so audacious, and a detail or two (such as "The Last Question" being a Foundation plant) wild enough to strain my suspension of disbelief, but it's so well executed I can't help but like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh that is fair, and it wasn't intended as a tower of babel analogue, I just sort of realized that layer was present while writing it.

I wanted it to be left open whether it has anything to do with humans per se. With all the aliens etc out there, and heck just the galaxies themselves flying around, Humans might not even be the target of it.

EDIT: in response to this and other feedback, I unredacted a few things, including what was being developed at the time that may have instigated things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Tower of Babel allegory is buried too deep to have a real significance. Because there are no real-world dates or names to provide context, I'm not feeling any sense of frisson from the idea that entropy was created by God to halt human progress.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my second SCP and while unrelated to my first…I'm seeing a pattern that maybe I'm subconsciously obsessed with the Tower of Babel story……maybe I should look into that…..

This was inspired by my nihilistic fear of the heat death of the universe, by http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-8900-ex, and by the Robert Frost poem, Mending Wall (from which I got the main series title….if that is inappropriate re: copyright I'll change the title to either Heat Death of the Universe or Thermodynamic Equilibrium or Second Law of Thermodynamics).

I still worry about whether the idea will grab people (or even make sense to people not already physics dorks), but I love:
-Heat Death of the Universe/Entropy as an SCP.
-The idea that something we readers think of as normal is actually the SCP and "historical in-world normal" is something we readers can barely comprehend. See for example http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-8900-ex
-Operation Gaslight.

My head cannon is that there is some omnipotent that has been putting up with all this universe wide growth and building but really wants things to simmer down and they have had enough, the wall is too damn high let it fall.

Oh, and in case you are wondering, there is no such thing as Carnot Convection…..anymore…..

Images come from wikicommons.
